name: praat-parselmouth

metadata.name: praat-parselmouth
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU General Public License v3 or later (GPLv3+)
Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules
metadata.summary: Praat in Python, the Pythonic way

documentation:
# Parselmouth - Praat in Python, the Pythonic way

**Parselmouth** is a Python library for the [Praat](http://www.praat.org)
software.

Though other attempts have been made at porting functionality from Praat to
Python, Parselmouth is unique in its aim to provide a complete and Pythonic
interface to the internal Praat code. While other projects either wrap Praat's
scripting language or reimplementing parts of Praat's functionality in Python,
Parselmouth directly accesses Praat's C/C++ code (which means the algorithms
and their output are exactly the same as in Praat) and provides efficient
access to the program's data, but *also* provides an interface that looks
no different from any other Python library.

Drop by our [Gitter chat room](https://gitter.im/PraatParselmouth/Lobby) or
post a message to our
[Google discussion group](https://groups.google.com/d/forum/parselmouth) if
you have any question, remarks, or requests!

More information on the **installation** and some basic **examples**
can be found on Parselmouth's GitHub repository:
https://github.com/YannickJadoul/Parselmouth

modules.name: extern.fmt.support.docopt
documentation:
Pythonic command-line interface parser that will make you smile.

* http://docopt.org
* Repository and issue-tracker: https://github.com/docopt/docopt
* Licensed under terms of MIT license (see LICENSE-MIT)
* Copyright (c) 2013 Vladimir Keleshev, vladimir@keleshev.com

functions.name: extern.fmt.support.docopt.transform
documentation:
Expand pattern into an (almost) equivalent one, but with single Either.

Example: ((-a | -b) (-c | -d)) => (-a -c | -a -d | -b -c | -b -d)
Quirks: [-a] => (-a), (-a...) => (-a -a)

functions.name: extern.fmt.support.docopt.parse_long
documentation: long ::= '--' chars [ ( ' ' | '=' ) chars ] ;

functions.name: extern.fmt.support.docopt.parse_shorts
documentation: shorts ::= '-' ( chars )* [ [ ' ' ] chars ] ;

functions.name: extern.fmt.support.docopt.parse_expr
documentation: expr ::= seq ( '|' seq )* ;

functions.name: extern.fmt.support.docopt.parse_atom
documentation:
atom ::= '(' expr ')' | '[' expr ']' | 'options'
| long | shorts | argument | command ;

functions.name: extern.fmt.support.docopt.parse_argv
extern.fmt.support.docopt.docopt
documentation:
Parse `argv` based on command-line interface described in `doc`.

`docopt` creates your command-line interface based on its
description that you pass as `doc`. Such description can contain
--options, \<positional-argument>, commands, which could be
[optional], (required), (mutually | exclusive) or repeated...

# Parameters

doc

: Description of your command-line interface.

argv

: Argument vector to be parsed. sys.argv[1:] is used if not
  provided.

help

: Set to False to disable automatic help on -h or --help
  options.

version

: If passed, the object will be printed if --version is in
  `argv`.

options_first

: Set to True to require options precede positional arguments,
  i.e. to forbid options and positional arguments intermix.

# Returns

args

: A dictionary, where keys are names of command-line elements
  such as e.g. "--verbose" and "\<path>", and values are the
  parsed values of those elements.

# Example

```pycon
>>> from docopt import docopt
>>> doc = '''
... Usage:
...     my_program tcp <host> <port> [--timeout=<seconds>]
...     my_program serial <port> [--baud=<n>] [--timeout=<seconds>]
...     my_program (-h | --help | --version)
...
... Options:
...     -h, --help  Show this screen and exit.
...     --baud=<n>  Baudrate [default: 9600]
... '''
>>> argv = ['tcp', '127.0.0.1', '80', '--timeout', '30']
>>> docopt(doc, argv)
{'--baud': '9600',
 '--help': False,
 '--timeout': '30',
 '--version': False,
 '<host>': '127.0.0.1',
 '<port>': '80',
 'serial': False,
 'tcp': True}
```

# See also

- For video introduction see <http://docopt.org>
- Full documentation is available in README.rst as well as online
  at <https://github.com/docopt/docopt#readme>

functions.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.has_flag
documentation:
Return the flag if a flag name is supported on the
specified compiler, otherwise None (can be used as a boolean).
If multiple flags are passed, return the first that matches.
cf http://bugs.python.org/issue26689

functions.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.auto_cpp_level
documentation: Return the max supported C++ std level (17, 14, or 11). Returns latest on Windows.

functions.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.naive_recompile
documentation:
This will recompile only if the source file changes. It does not check
header files, so a more advanced function or Ccache is better if you have
editable header files in your package.

functions.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.no_recompile
documentation:
This is the safest but slowest choice (and is the default) - will always
recompile sources.

classes.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.Pybind11Extension

methods.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.Pybind11Extension._add_cflags
documentation: flags are prepended, so that they can be further overridden, e.g. by
``extra_compile_args=["-g"]``.

methods.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.Pybind11Extension.cxx_std
documentation:
The CXX standard level. If set, will add the required flags. If left
at 0, it will trigger an automatic search when pybind11's build_ext
is used. If None, will have no effect.  Besides just the flags, this
may add a register warning/error fix for Python 2 or macos-min 10.9
or 10.14.

Build a C++11+ Extension module with pybind11. This automatically adds the
recommended flags when you init the extension and assumes C++ sources - you
can further modify the options yourself.

The customizations are:

- `/EHsc` and `/bigobj` on Windows
- `stdlib=libc++` on macOS
- `visibility=hidden` and `-g0` on Unix

Finally, you can set `cxx_std` via constructor or afterwords to enable
flags for C++ std, and a few extra helper flags related to the C++ standard
level. It is \_highly\_ recommended you either set this, or use the provided
`build_ext`, which will search for the highest supported extension for
you if the `cxx_std` property is not set. Do not set the `cxx_std`
property more than once, as flags are added when you set it. Set the
property to None to disable the addition of C++ standard flags.

If you want to add pybind11 headers manually, for example for an exact
git checkout, then set `include_pybind11=False`.

Warning: do not use property-based access to the instance on Python 2 -
this is an ugly old-style class due to Distutils.
It is recommended to use PEP 518 builds if using this module. However, this
file explicitly supports being copied into a user's project directory
standalone, and pulling pybind11 with the deprecated setup_requires feature.
If you copy the file, remember to add it to your MANIFEST.in, and add the current
directory into your path if it sits beside your setup.py.

classes.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.build_ext

methods.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.build_ext.build_extensions
documentation: Build extensions, injecting C++ std for Pybind11Extension if needed.

Customized build_ext that allows an auto-search for the highest supported
C++ level for Pybind11Extension. This is only needed for the auto-search
for now, and is completely optional otherwise.

classes.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.ParallelCompile

methods.name: pybind11.pybind11.setup_helpers.ParallelCompile.function
documentation: Builds a function object usable as distutils.ccompiler.CCompiler.compile.

Make a parallel compile function. Inspired by
numpy.distutils.ccompiler.CCompiler_compile and cppimport.

This takes several arguments that allow you to customize the compile
function created:

envvar:

: Set an environment variable to control the compilation threads, like
  NPY_NUM_BUILD_JOBS

default:

: 0 will automatically multithread, or 1 will only multithread if the
  envvar is set.

max:

: The limit for automatic multithreading if non-zero

needs_recompile:

: A function of (obj, src) that returns True when recompile is needed. No
  effect in isolated mode; use ccache instead, see
  <https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/1507/>

To use:

```
ParallelCompile("NPY_NUM_BUILD_JOBS").install()
```

or:

```
with ParallelCompile("NPY_NUM_BUILD_JOBS"):
    setup(...)
```

By default, this assumes all files need to be recompiled. A smarter
function can be provided via needs_recompile. If the output has not yet
been generated, the compile will always run, and this function is not
called.
Optional parallel compile utility
inspired by: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/11013851/speeding-up-build-process-with-distutils
and: https://github.com/tbenthompson/cppimport/blob/stable/cppimport/build_module.py
and NumPy's parallel distutils module:
https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/numpy/distutils/ccompiler.py

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_eigen.test_negative_stride_from_python
documentation:
Eigen doesn't support (as of yet) negative strides. When a function takes an Eigen matrix by
copy or const reference, we can pass a numpy array that has negative strides.  Otherwise, an
exception will be thrown as Eigen will not be able to map the numpy array.

documentation: Tests various ways of returning references and non-referencing copies

documentation: Tests Eigen's ability to mutate numpy values

documentation: Tests numpy mutating Eigen matrices (for returned Eigen::Ref<...>s)

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_eigen.test_both_ref_mutators
documentation: Tests a complex chain of nested eigen/numpy references

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_eigen.test_eigen_ref_life_support
documentation:
Ensure the lifetime of temporary arrays created by the `Ref` caster

The `Ref` caster sometimes creates a copy which needs to stay alive. This needs to
happen both for directs casts (just the array) or indirectly (e.g. list of arrays).

documentation: Tests returning an offset (non-first MI) base class pointer to a derived instance

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_multiple_inheritance.test_diamond_inheritance
documentation: Tests that diamond inheritance works as expected (issue #959)

documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

modules.name: pybind11.tests.test_factory_constructors
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_factory_constructors.test_init_factory_basic
documentation: Tests py::init_factory() wrapper around various ways of returning the object

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_factory_constructors.test_init_factory_casting
documentation: Tests py::init_factory() wrapper with various upcasting and downcasting returns

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_factory_constructors.test_init_factory_alias
documentation: Tests py::init_factory() wrapper with value conversions and alias types

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_factory_constructors.test_init_factory_dual
documentation: Tests init factory functions with dual main/alias factory functions

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_factory_constructors.test_no_placement_new
documentation:
Prior to 2.2, `py::init<...>` relied on the type supporting placement
new; this tests a class without placement new support.

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_factory_constructors.test_reallocation_a
documentation:
When the constructor is overloaded, previous overloads can require a preallocated value.
This test makes sure that such preallocated values only happen when they might be necessary,
and that they are deallocated properly.

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_factory_constructors.test_invalid_self
documentation:
Tests invocation of the pybind-registered base class with an invalid `self` argument.  You
can only actually do this on Python 3: Python 2 raises an exception itself if you try.

documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

modules.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_vector
documentation: std::vector <-> list

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_deque
documentation: std::deque <-> list

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_array
documentation: std::array <-> list

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_valarray
documentation: std::valarray <-> list

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_map
documentation: std::map <-> dict

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_set
documentation: std::set <-> set

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_recursive_casting
documentation: Tests that stl casters preserve lvalue/rvalue context for container values

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_move_out_container
documentation:
Properties use the `reference_internal` policy by default. If the underlying function
returns an rvalue, the policy is automatically changed to `move` to avoid referencing
a temporary. In case the return value is a container of user-defined types, the policy
also needs to be applied to the elements, not just the container.

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_vec_of_reference_wrapper
documentation: #171: Can't return reference wrappers (or STL structures containing them)

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_stl_pass_by_pointer
documentation: Passing nullptr or None to an STL container pointer is not expected to work

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_missing_header_message
documentation:
Trying convert `list` to a `std::vector`, or vice versa, without including
<pybind11/stl.h> should result in a helpful suggestion in the error message

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_function_with_string_and_vector_string_arg
documentation:
Check if a string is NOT implicitly converted to a list, which was the
behavior before fix of issue #1258

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_stl.test_issue_1561
documentation: check fix for issue #1561

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_class.test_qualname
documentation:
Tests that a properly qualified name is set in __qualname__ (even in pre-3.3, where we
backport the attribute) and that generated docstrings properly use it and the module name

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_class.test_override_static
documentation: #511: problem with inheritance + overwritten def_static

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_class.test_implicit_conversion_life_support

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_class.test_operator_new_delete
documentation: Tests that class-specific operator new/delete functions are invoked

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_class.test_bind_protected_functions
documentation: Expose protected member functions to Python using a helper class

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_class.test_brace_initialization
documentation: Tests that simple POD classes can be constructed using C++11 brace initialization

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_class.test_class_refcount
documentation: Instances must correctly increase/decrease the reference count of their types (#1029)

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_class.test_final
documentation: https://foss.heptapod.net/pypy/pypy/-/issues/2742

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_class.test_non_final_final
documentation: https://foss.heptapod.net/pypy/pypy/-/issues/2742

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_unicode_conversion
documentation: Tests unicode conversion and error reporting.

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_single_char_arguments
documentation: Tests failures for passing invalid inputs to char-accepting functions

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_bytes_to_string
documentation:
Tests the ability to pass bytes to C++ string-accepting functions.  Note that this is
one-way: the only way to return bytes to Python is via the pybind11::bytes class.

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_string_view
documentation: Tests support for C++17 string_view arguments and return values

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_integer_casting
documentation: Issue #929 - out-of-range integer values shouldn't be accepted

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_tuple
documentation: std::pair <-> tuple & std::tuple <-> tuple

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_builtins_cast_return_none
documentation: Casters produced with PYBIND11_TYPE_CASTER() should convert nullptr to None

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_none_deferred
documentation: None passed as various argument types should defer to other overloads

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_reference_wrapper
documentation: std::reference_wrapper for builtin and user types

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_complex_cast
documentation: std::complex casts

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_bool_caster
documentation: Test bool caster implicit conversions.

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_int_long
documentation:
In Python 2, a C++ int should return a Python int rather than long
if possible: longs are not always accepted where ints are used (such
as the argument to sys.exit()). A C++ long long is always a Python
long.

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_builtin_casters.test_const_ref_caster
documentation:
Verifies that const-ref is propagated through type_caster cast_op.
The returned ConstRefCasted type is a mimimal type that is constructed to
reference the casting mode used.

documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

documentation: https://foss.heptapod.net/pypy/pypy/-/issues/2444

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_buffers.test_inherited_protocol
documentation: SquareMatrix is derived from Matrix and inherits the buffer protocol

modules.name: pybind11.tests.test_operator_overloading
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_operator_overloading.test_operators_notimplemented
documentation: #393: need to return NotSupported to ensure correct arithmetic operator behavior

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_operator_overloading.test_nested
documentation: #328: first member in a class can't be used in operators

modules.name: pybind11.tests.test_gil_scoped
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_gil_scoped._run_in_process
documentation: Runs target in process and returns its exitcode after 10s (None if still alive).

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_gil_scoped._python_to_cpp_to_python
documentation: Calls different C++ functions that come back to Python.

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_gil_scoped._python_to_cpp_to_python_from_threads
documentation: Calls different C++ functions that come back to Python, from Python threads.

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_gil_scoped.test_python_to_cpp_to_python_from_thread
documentation:
Makes sure there is no GIL deadlock when running in a thread.

It runs in a separate process to be able to stop and assert if it deadlocks.
TODO: FIXME, sometimes returns -11 (segfault) instead of 0 on macOS Python 3.9

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_gil_scoped.test_python_to_cpp_to_python_from_thread_multiple_parallel
documentation:
Makes sure there is no GIL deadlock when running in a thread multiple times in parallel.

It runs in a separate process to be able to stop and assert if it deadlocks.
TODO: FIXME on macOS Python 3.9

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_gil_scoped.test_python_to_cpp_to_python_from_thread_multiple_sequential
documentation:
Makes sure there is no GIL deadlock when running in a thread multiple times sequentially.

It runs in a separate process to be able to stop and assert if it deadlocks.
TODO: FIXME on macOS Python 3.9

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_gil_scoped.test_python_to_cpp_to_python_from_process
documentation:
Makes sure there is no GIL deadlock when using processes.

This test is for completion, but it was never an issue.
TODO: FIXME on macOS Python 3.9

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_gil_scoped.test_cross_module_gil
documentation: Makes sure that the GIL can be acquired by another module from a GIL-released state.

modules.name: pybind11.tests.test_virtual_functions
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_virtual_functions.test_alias_delay_initialization1
documentation:
`A` only initializes its trampoline class when we inherit from it

If we just create and use an A instance directly, the trampoline initialization is
bypassed and we only initialize an A() instead (for performance reasons).

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_virtual_functions.test_alias_delay_initialization2
documentation:
`A2`, unlike the above, is configured to always initialize the alias

While the extra initialization and extra class layer has small virtual dispatch
performance penalty, it also allows us to do more things with the trampoline
class such as defining local variables and performing construction/destruction.

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_virtual_functions.test_move_support
documentation: PyPy: Reference count > 1 causes call with noncopyable instance
to fail in ncv1.print_nc()

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_virtual_functions.test_dispatch_issue
documentation: #159: virtual function dispatch has problems with similar-named functions

functions.name: pybind11.tests.test_virtual_functions.test_override_ref
documentation: #392/397: overriding reference-returning functions

modules.name: praat.external.gsl.flatten_gsl
documentation: !/usr/bin/python
Makes the directory structure of gsl flat by renaming all files and references by #include
E.g. the file 'd/a.c' will be renamed as: gsl_d__a.c
djmw 20100223 New makefile layout

functions.name: praat.external.gsl.flatten_gsl.write_to_file
documentation: per file: [type inmakefile indist renamed_as ntimesincluded headerreplacement]
type = c h

functions.name: praat.external.gsl.flatten_gsl.process_files
documentation: #include .....
rewrite <gsl/file.h> to "file.h"
<file.h> -> <file.h>
"file.(c|h)" -> "gsl_dir__file.(c|h)"
